# Nanozyme with Photo-Enhanced Dual Enzyme-Like Activities for Deep Pancreatic Cancer Therapy


Shanshan Li, #[a] Lu Shang, #[b] Bolong Xu, [a] Shunhao Wang, [c]  Kai Gu, [a] Qingyuan Wu, [a]  Yun Sun, [a] Qinghua Zhang, [d]  Hailong Yang, [a] Fengrong Zhang, [a]  Lin Gu, [d] Tierui Zhang,* [b] and Huiyu Liu* [a]

Abstract: Compared with natural enzymes, nanozymes have attracted extensive interests due to their high stability, low cost and easy preparation, especially in the field of cancer therapy. However, the relatively  low  catalytic  activity  of  nanozymes  in  the  tumor microenvironment (TME) limited their applications. Herein, we report a novel nanozyme (PtFe@Fe3O4) with dual enzyme-like activities for highly  efficient  tumor  catalytic  therapy.  PtFe@Fe3O4shows  the intrinsic photothermal effect as well as photo-enhanced peroxidaselike and catalase-like activities in the acidic TME, thereby effectively killing tumor cells and overcoming the tumor hypoxia. Importantly, a possible photo-enhanced synergistic catalytic mechanism of PtFe@Fe3O4was  first  disclosed.  We  believe  that  this  work  will advance the development of nanozymes in tumor catalytic therapy.


# Introduction


Nanozymes are enzyme mimics with both unique properties of nanomaterials  and  catalytic  activities  of  natural  enzymes.  In recent years, they have been rapidly developed in biosensing, [1-3] antibacterial [4,5] and immunoassay [6]  due to their high stability, low cost  and  easy  preparation. [7,8]   Especially,  they  showed  great promises  in  reactive  oxygen  species  (ROS)-mediated  tumor therapy. [9,10] However, the highly complex tumor microenvironment (TME) leads to an insufficient catalytic efficiency of  nanozymes,  and  makes  it  difficult  to  achieve satisfactory therapeutic effects. Hence, it is urgent but challenging to develop an efficient nanozyme-based TME-responsive strategy for highly potent tumor catalytic therapy.

TME is generally known to feature unique characteristics such as the mildly acidic nature, hypoxia, and overproduced hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (50-100×10 -6M). [11,12]  Tumor hypoxia not only reduces therapeutic effects but also induces tumor proliferation and  metastasis. [13,14]   To  solve  this  problem,  H2O2-responsive nanozymes were explored for tumor therapy. [16-19]  According to current studies, H2O2could be decomposed into highly toxic ROS by peroxidase (POD) mimics under acidic condition or H2O and O2by  catalase (CAT)  mimics  under  neutral  condition. [20,21] However, nanozymes possessing both POD-like activity and CAT

Another strategy to achieve effective tumor catalytic therapy is to improve the catalytic activity of nanozymes. Hindered by the limited H2O2concentration in TME, the effect of single nanozymebased catalytic therapy is not competent enough compared with combination therapy. Encouraged by the development of phototherapy, [22,23] the introduction of the near-infrared (NIR) laser is  considered  as  a  promising  strategy  to  promote  the  catalytic activity and therapeutic effect of nanozymes. On the one hand, studies  have  been  reported  that  nanocatalysts  could  promote ROS production under light irradiation by direct electron transfer and photo-enhanced Fenton reaction. [24]  On the other hand, the ROS generation can be significantly improved by the photothermal  effect  of  nanozymes. [25,26]   Therefore,  the  photoenhanced catalytic activity and photothermal effect of nanozymes are  expected  to  achieve  the  balanced  combination  of  tumor catalytic therapy and photothermal therapy (PTT).

Scheme1. Schematic illustration of the photo-enhanced tumor catalytic therapy for PtFe@Fe3O4nanozyme.

Herein, in response to TME, we constructed a nanozyme with dual  enzyme-like  activities  (PtFe@Fe3O4)  for  highly  efficient tumor  catalytic  therapy  (Scheme1).  Serving  as  an  H2O2responsive nanozyme, PtFe@Fe3O4exhibited both intrinsic PODlike and CAT-like activities under acidic condition. Importantly, the catalytic  activities  of  PtFe@Fe3O4were  significantly  enhanced under the irradiation of the NIR laser. Meanwhile, for in vivo deep tumor therapy, we  used  the light interventional device to implement the in-situ photo-enhanced catalytic activities strategy. Combining the excellent photothermal effect, PtFe@Fe3O4nanozyme  could  effectively  overcome  tumor  hypoxia  and  kill tumor  cells  with  a  tumor  inhibition  rate  of99.8%  for  deep pancreatic cancer. Moreover, to better understand the catalytic reactions of PtFe@Fe3O4nanozyme, we for the first time revealed the electron transfer process among PtFe nanorods (NRs), Fe3O4nanoparticles (NPs) and H2O2molecules. We believe this work

will  open  a  new  horizon  of  exploring  novel  nanozyme-based strategies for tumor catalytic therapy.


# Results and Discussion


It was reported that the electron states of Pt could significantly be modulated  by  alloying with  Fe. [27]   Hence,  we  used  PtFe  alloy NRs [28] with  less  platinum  but  an  improved  activity  for  H2O2activation to construct the PtFe@Fe3O4NRs. First, the PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  were  synthesized by  assembling  multiple uniform Fe3O4NPs on the surface of the as-prepared PtFe NRs. Transmission  electron  microscopic  (TEM)  images  showed  that Fe3O4NPs with a diameter of mainly5-20nm attached to ultrafine PtFe NRs with a length of about140nm and width about2nm (Figure1a  and  Figure  S1-a  and  b).  The  lattice  fringes  with  a spacing  of0.250nm  and0.201nm  could  be  observed  and ascribed to (311) planes [29]  of Fe3O4NPs and (111) planes of PtFe NRs, respectively,  indicated  by  scanning  transmission  electron microscopy (STEM) (Figure1b). Scanning transmission electron microscopy-energy dispersive X-ray (STEM-EDX)  elemental mapping and STEM-EDX line scan were performed to confirm the composition of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs. The distributions of Pt is even through the whole nanorods (Figure1c-d). And the distribution of Fe and O elements are in the same particle, which also suggested the attachment of Fe3O4NPs on the surface of PtFe NRs. The presence peaks for Pt, Fe, O elements indicated the formation of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs (Figure1e), which was consistent with TEM and EDX results (Figure S2). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed to determine the fine structure of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs. One distinct diffraction peak assigned to (111) plane at40.28 ° for PtFe NRs was observed (Figure1f). In the case of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs, five characteristic  peaks at30.55 ° ,  35.60 ° ,  43.53 ° ,  57.54 ° , 62.92 ° were also exhibited, which assigned to (220), (311), (400), (511), (440) planes of Fe3O4NPs, respectively. According to the inductively  coupled  plasma  mass  spectrometer  (ICP-MS),  the mass  ratio  of  Pt  and  Fe  in  PtFe  NRs  was49:51,  and  the percentage  of  PtFe  and  Fe3O4in  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  were estimated to be32wt% and68wt%, respectively (Table S1).

The  photothermal  property  of  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  was  further investigated. UV-vis-NIR  absorbance  spectra showed  that PtFe@Fe3O4NRs displayed a broad absorption from the visible to the NIR  region (Figure1g), indicating the potential of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs for photothermal conversion under NIR laser irradiation.  It  was  noted  that  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  exhibited  a concentration-dependent temperature increase after exposure to NIR laser (Figure1h). The temperature rose to59.4 ° C under the concentration  of100  μg  mL -1 ,  while  no  significant  temperature variation  was  observed  for  the  PBS  solution.  Moreover,  the infrared  thermal  images  also  showed  the  rapid  temperature increase  of  PtFe@Fe3O4compared  to  that  of  PBS  solution (Figure S3). The excellent photothermal stability of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs was revealed via laser on-off cycles (Figure1i). In addition, the  photothermal properties of  PtFe  NRs and Fe3O4NPs were also  investigated  (Figure  S4).  The  elevated  temperature  of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs suspension (29.2 ° C) was slightly lower than that of PtFe NRs (31.5 ° C), [30]  which may be due to the Fe3O4NPs in PtFe@Fe3O4NRs (named as Fe3O4|PtFe@Fe3O4) blocking the light,  thus  reducing  the  photo-absorption  of  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs. The photothermal conversion efficiencies of  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs,

PtFe NRs, Fe3O4NPs were calculated to be7.6%, 14.9%, 3.8%, respectively. [31]   (Table  S2).  These  results  demonstrated  the reasonable  photothermal-conversion  efficiency  of  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs.

Figure1. Characterizations of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs. ( a ) TEM  image of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs. ( b, c ) STEM images of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs. ( d ) STEM-EDX elemental  mapping  images  of  O,  Fe,  and  Pt.  ( e )  STEM-EDX  line  scan  of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs. ( f )  XRD patterns of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs and PtFe NRs. ( g ) UV-vis-NIR absorption  spectra of  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs, PtFe  NRs,  and Fe3O4NPs. ( h ) Temperature measurement of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs solution with different concentrations (0, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200 μg mL -1 ) under808nm laser irradiation. ( i ) Real-time temperature measurement of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs under808nm laser irradiation for4cycles.

The POD-like activities of  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs, PtFe NRs, and Fe3O4NPs were firstly investigated by the oxidation of3,3′,5,5′tetramethylbenzidine  (TMB)  in  the  presence  of  H2O2.  Both PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  and  PtFe  NRs  showed  obvious  POD-like activity,  and  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  exhibited  the  highest  catalytic activity (Figure2a). The optimal temperature and pH  for PtFe@Fe3O4nanozyme ranged from35to45 ° C and2.5to6.5, respectively  (Figure  S5and  S6).  Moreover,  the  steady-state kinetic parameters including the Michaelis-Menten constant ( K M) and the maximum initial velocity ( Vmax) were acquired to assess their catalytic ability (Figure S7). [32]  The K M of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs were determined to be53.55mM for H2O2substrate and0.213mM  for  TMB  substrate,  indicating  the  comparable  affinity  of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  for  H2O2and  TMB  substrates  with  that  of natural horseradish peroxidase (3.70mM for H2O2substrate and0.434mM for TMB substrate). [33]  Importantly, the Vmax  value  of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs for H2O2showed1.3-fold increases relative to Fe3O4NPs (Table S3). These results demonstrated that PtFe@Fe3O4NRs could effectively promote the decomposition of H2O2and enhance the catalytic oxidation of TMB.

To  investigate  whether  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  possess  CAT-like activity under acidic condition, we tested the generation of O2in the presence of H2O2and PtFe@Fe3O4NRs. It is worth noting that  no  significant  difference  was  observed  for  control  groups, while  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  could  produce  more  O2molecules (Figure2d). Compared with Fe3O4NPs, the generation amount of O2by PtFe@Fe3O4NRs showed1.4-fold increases, demonstrating that the combination of PtFe NRs and Fe3O4NPs effectively  promoted  the  catalytic  performance  of  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs.

According  to  the  Arrhenius  formula,  the  surface  plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of noble metal could effectively convert NIR light energy to heat energy, which could promote the catalytic reaction. [34]   More  importantly,  the  SPR  effect  of  PtFe  NRs  is associated with the enhancement of the electric near-field in the vicinity. Therefore, we hypothesized that the SPR effect of PtFe in PtFe@Fe3O4NRs (denoted as PtFe|PtFe@Fe3O4) could also inhibit the rapid recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs and boost the excitation of electron-hole pairs in Fe3O4|PtFe@Fe3O4, [35] thereby H2O2can accept electrons facilely. [36]   The  SPR  effect  was  investigated  by  observing  the catalytic  performance of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs under different light wavelength (Figure S8). [37]  Based on this, we first tested if the808nm laser could enhance the POD-like activity of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs. After exposing to808nm laser, the POD-like activity of both the PtFe NRs and PtFe@Fe3O4NRs significantly promoted while no enhanced catalytic activity was detected for Fe3O4NPs (Figure2b). And the POD-like activity of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs exhibited a time-dependent  irradiation  manner  under808nm  laser  (Figure2c). The effect of SPR and thermal on the catalytic process were also  compared.  The  result  showed  that  both  the  SPR  and  the thermal effect  could  accelerate  the  catalytic  reaction,  while  the effect of temperature on the catalytic process was much higher. (Figure S9). In addition, the photo-enhanced CAT-like activity was also observed (Figure2e). After exposing to808nm laser, the generation  amount  of  O2by  PtFe  NRs  and  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs showed1.5-fold and1.2-fold increases, respectively, compared with Fe3O4NPs. These results indicated that PtFe NRs played a vital role in the photo-enhanced catalytic system.

To elucidate the catalytic mechanism of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs, we first  determined  the  H2O2decomposition  efficiency  during  the catalytic  process  by  using  ammonium  molybdate.  The  result indicated that the concentration of H2O2significantly decreased after  incubation  with  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  under808nm  laser


# RESEARCH ARTICLE


irradiation, suggesting  the  H2O2could  be  decomposed  by PtFe@Fe3O4NRs (Figure2f). To further identify the generated ROS, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were used as direct evidence  for  the  generated  ROS.  5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-Noxide (DMPO) was considered as a trapping probe for hydroxyl radical (·OH) and superoxide anion radical (O2 · -).  After  the1.5min  and3min808nm  laser  irradiation,  these  ESR  intensities were markedly increased (Figure2g and Figure S10). Therefore, PtFe@Fe3O4NRs indeed have the potential to decompose H2O2and  exhibit  the  photo-enhanced  generation  of  ·OH  and  O2 · -, showing their high catalytic activities.

We then investigated the chemical composition and chemical states  of  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  to  explore  the  internal  catalytic mechanism  by  using  X-ray  photoelectron  spectroscopy  (XPS) measurements. Two peaks at the binding energy of75.1eV and71.8eV could be assigned to Pt4f5/2and Pt4f7/2, respectively (Figure2h). [38,39]  Compared with single Pt NPs, the binding energy of Pt4f in PtFe NRs and PtFe@Fe3O4NRs showed noticeable negative  shifts  (~0.6eV  and  ~0.8eV,  respectively),  which revealed that the electrons were transferred from Fe atoms to Pt atoms, [40]  resulting in an electron-rich state of Pt. The surface Pt in PtFe NRs and PtFe@Fe3O4NRs were proven to be mainly in the metallic state. In addition, the XPS characteristics for Fe2p peak  (Figure2i  and  Figure  S11)  showed  two  distinct  curves, corresponding to Fe3+  and Fe2+ , respectively, which revealed that Fe atoms in PtFe NRs and PtFe@Fe3O4NRs were mainly in the oxidized state. These  results  suggested  that  the  reduction reaction process had more chances to happen on the surface of PtFe|PtFe@Fe3O4and the oxidation reaction happened on the Fe|PtFe and Fe3O4|PtFe@Fe3O4.

Based on the above experimental results, a possible catalytic mechanism  was  proposed.  During  the  catalytic  process  of PtFe@Fe3O4nanozyme, Fe3O4|PtFe@Fe3O4acts as an electronic pump that draws electrons from H2O2 [41]  and constantly transports  electrons  to  Pt|PtFe@Fe3O4.  Then,  the  electron-rich state of Pt|PtFe@Fe3O4was formed, thus H2O2can easily obtain electrons  from  the  surface  Pt|PtFe@Fe3O4and  generate  ·OH under  acidic  condition,  referring  to  Equation  (1).  Meanwhile, electron  flowed  from  Fe3O4|PtFe@Fe3O4to  PtFe|PtFe@Fe3O4and  the  exposed  Fe3+   promotes  the  second  reaction  to  occurr effectively, as described in Equation (2). Then, Fe3+  reacts with O2 · to produce O2 (3). [17]

In addition, under the NIR laser irradiation, the photothermalaugmented effect could accelerate the above catalytic reactions of  PtFe@Fe3O4nanozyme.  Moreover,  the  NIR  laser  could promote the generation of high-energy electrons in PtFe|PtFe@Fe3O4, and accelerate the excitation of electron-hole in  Fe3O4|PtFe@Fe3O4, [42]   which  leads  to  the  observed  photoenhanced POD and CAT-like activities of PtFe@Fe3O4nanozyme. To ensure that PtFe@Fe3O4NRs could be effectively uptaken by cancer cells, the endocytosis efficiency of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs was firstly investigated by Bio-TEM and ICP-MS (Figure S12and S13).

Results  showed  that  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  could  be  efficiently internalized  with  increased  incubation  time.  Then,  the in  vitro cytotoxicity and biosafety of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs were evaluated. After  incubation  with  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  for24h  and48h, concentration-dependent cytotoxicity could be observed and the cell viability for the24h group of the human pancreatic cancer cell line SW1990was below65% under the concentration of100 μg mL -1   (Figure3a).  This  result  indicated  that  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs could produce a large amount of ·OH at the cellular level, which can  increase  lipid  peroxidation  of  membranes  and  inhibit  the function of proteins and even destroy nucleic acids and chromosomes  under  acidic  condition. [43]   Meanwhile,  the  cell toxicity effect of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs on normal human pancreatic duct epithelial cell line HPDE6-C7was further investigated. The cell viability remained at77% even at the dose of200 μg mL -1 , which  revealed  the  proper  biosafety  of  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  for normal  human  pancreatic  duct  epithelial  cells  (Figure  S14).

The hypoxic condition results in the increased invasiveness and metastasis of tumors  and  the enhancement  of  antioxidant capacity. [13]  Thus, improving the hypoxic condition could reduce the antioxidative ability of tumor cells and enhance the oxidative damage. As shown in Figure3b, the cells treated with normoxia (65.8%)  showed  much  higher  inhibition  rate  than  that  treated under hypoxia (33.8%) at the concentration of100 μg mL -1 . After exposing to808nm laser, the photo-induced killing efficiency of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs at normoxic condition reached to80.2% at the concentration  of100  μg  mL -1 .  These  results  indicated  that PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  possessed  an  excellent in vitro catalytic therapeutic effect.

To  visually  observe  the  living  and  dead  cells,  SW1990cells were  stained  with  Calcein-AM  and  propidium  iodide  (PI)  after incubation  with  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  (100  μg  mL -1 ).  The  red fluorescence  (dead  cells)  and  green  fluorescence  (living  cells) could  be  distinguished  by  confocal  laser  scanning  microscopy (CLSM)  (Figure3c).  The  dead  cells  in  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs-NIR group were much more than those in PtFe@Fe3O4NRs group, which  demonstrated  the  significant  photo-enhanced  therapy efficiency. In addition, significant damage of cell membrane and nucleus could be seen in PtFe@Fe3O4NRs-NIR group due to the photothermal effet (Figure3d and Figure S15). To investigate the intracellular nanozyme properties, the intracellular ROS level was detected.  As  shown  in  Figure3e,  bright  green  fluorescence signals could be observed in PtFe@Fe3O4NRs and PtFe@Fe3O4NRs-NIR groups. In contrast, merely no green fluorescence signal could  be  found  in  other  groups.  The  results  of  flow  cytometry analysis of the intracellular ROS (Figure S16-17) were consistent with CLSM images. For specific detection of ·OH at the cellular level,  hydroxyphenyl  fluorescein  (HPF)  was  used  (Figure  S18). More ·OH were detected under the stimulation of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs and NIR laser. [44,45]  These results indicated that PtFe@Fe3O4NRs could effectively exert  POD-like activity in tumor cells and inhibit cell growth.

Considering the mode of intravenous administration, the effect of  PtFe@Fe3O4on  red  blood  cells  was  first  investigated.  The hemolysis rate of PtFe@Fe3O4nanozyme was < 5% even at the concentration of200 μg mL -1 , suggesting the good biosafety of PtFe@Fe3O4  (Figure  S19).  To  evaluate  the in  vivo potential therapeutic effects of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs for pancreatic cancer, an in-situ pancreatic cancer mouse model was established. And the aggregation  of  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  in  the  tumor  sites  was  first evaluated  by  photoacoustic  (PA)  imaging  and  X-ray  computed tomography (CT) imaging. After intravenous injection of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  both  CT  and  PA  signal  intensities  were significantly improved  in the tumor  sites and  reached  the maximum at1h (Figure4a-d and Figure S20-22). The reason for


# RESEARCH ARTICLE


the rapid accumulation of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs at the tumor site may be induced by the unique linear structure and ultra-small size of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs.  These  results  revealed  the  potential  of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs as a dual PA / CT imaging contrast agents. More importantly, the tumor hypoxic condition was improved after intravenous injection (Figure4e-h). To further explore the improvement  of  hypoxic  condition  of  the  tumor  site,  hypoxiainducible factor (HIF)-1α staining assay was employed. [46] Compared with PBS and PBS-NIR groups, the HIF-1α expression in PtFe@Fe3O4and PtFe@Fe3O4-NIR groups were significantly reduced, which revealed the improved hypoxic condition (Figure4i).  The  generated  O2in  the  tumor  site  could  reduce  the antioxidant  capacity,  which  may  induce  tumor cells  to  be  more sensitive to free radicals, thus enhancing the therapeutic effect.

Figure5. In vivo photo-enhanced therapeutic effect of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs. ( a ) Real-time temperature changes of the tumor in SW1990tumor-bearing mice. ( b )  Infrared  thermal  images  of  the  tumor-bearing  mice.  ( c )  Tumor volume of mice in different groups during15days of treatments. ( d )  BLI  was utilized to continuously monitor the tumor region at3days interval in different groups. ( e ) Body weight of mice in different groups during15days of treatments.  Mean values and error bars are defined as mean and s.d., respectively. ( f ) H&E stain assay of tumor tissues in different groups. Scale bar=300 μm.

Then,  the in  vivo therapy  efficacy  of  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  was investigated. Firstly, the in vivo biocompatibility of  PtFe@Fe3O4was investigated. The results suggested that PtFe@Fe3O4has no long-term in  vivo toxicity  (Figure  S23).  SW1990tumor-bearing BALB/c nude mice were randomly divided into four groups (n=8): PBS group, PBS-NIR group, PtFe@Fe3O4group and PtFe@Fe3O4-NIR group. An NIR laser fiber with a custom hollow percutaneous  transhepatic  cholangiography  needle  penetrated deeply into the abdominal cavity of mice and irradiated the in situ pancreatic tumor. The PTT was implemented by using an infrared thermal mapping apparatus. A rapid rise of temperature could be observed in the PtFe@Fe3O4-NIR group (53 ° C). While, the PBSNIR  group  only  rose  to42  ° C  (Figure5a-b).  Bioluminescence imaging  (BLI)  was  utilized  to  monitor  the  growth  of  the  tumor (Figure5c-d). No significant enlargement of the tumor area was observed in the PtFe@Fe3O4group. Quantitative measurement data indicated that the tumor volume of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs-NIR group were significantly lower than those of other groups, and the tumor  inhibition  rate  for  the  four  groups  (PtFe@Fe3O4-NIR, PtFe@Fe3O4, PBS-NIR, PBS) are99.8%, 70.7%, 11.1%, and0%, respectively.  The  body  weight  of  each  group  was  monitored during  the15days  of  the  treatment  procedure  (Figure5e). Compared with the other three groups, a slight increase of the body weight was recorded of PtFe@Fe3O4-NIR group, demonstrating the improved life quality of the pancreatic tumorbearing  mice.  Hematoxylin  and  eosin  (H&E)  staining  was  also performed.  In  both  NIR  groups,  significant  cellular  damage  in cancerous tissues could be observed (Figure5f), and the area of dead cells in PtFe@Fe3O4-NIR group was much larger than that of PBS-NIR group. Moreover, there was no obvious damage in normal tissues, except for an occasional liver metastasis in the PBS-NIR group. Two mice in the PBS-NIR group developed liver metastasis  after  treatment  and  no  liver  metastases  in  other groups. (Figure S24).


# Conclusion


In  summary, we successfully designed  a novel nanozyme with photo-enhanced dual enzyme-like activities in response to TME. Combining with the photothermal effect, it achieved efficient deep pancreatic cancer therapy. More importantly, the possible catalytic  mechanisms  for  the  enhanced  POD-like  activity  and CAT-like activity were revealed. The  synthesis method  of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs effectively realized the redistribution of electrons  in  two  different  parts  (PtFe  NRs  and  Fe3O4NPs), resulting in the electron-rich state of Pt atoms. And the Fe3O4|PtFe@Fe3O4acted  as  an  electronic  pump  that  drew electrons from H2O2molecules and constantly transported electrons  to  Pt|PtFe@Fe3O4,  which  led  to  the  enhancement  of catalytic  activities.  In  addition,  with  a  wide  range  of  absorption wavelength, PtFe@Fe3O4NRs could serve as a bimodal contrast agent for CT and PA imaging, which could provide a multimodal imaging-guided  diagnosis  and  treatment  platform.  The  photoenhanced enzyme-like activities could effectively overcome tumor hypoxia  and  inhibit  tumor  growth,  resulting  in  high-efficiency therapeutic  effect  for  deep  pancreatic  cancer.  We  believe  that these  kind  of  metal  /  metal  oxide  nanomaterials  with  excellent enzyme-like  activities  can  provide  a  new  perspective  for  the design of nanomedicines.

# Experimental Procedures


Materials: Pt(acac)2 (99%) and Fe(CO)5 (99%) were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Oleylamine, Octadecene (ODE), oleic acid (OA), cyclohexane, ethylacetate were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company, Beijing, China. PEG-vitamin E (PEG-VE) (1kD) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. TMB liquid substrate system was purchased from Aladdin. Ammonium molybdate and DMPO spintrapping  adduct  were  purchased  from  Shanghai  Macklin  Biochemical  Co.,  Ltd.  3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay, acetoxymethylester of calcein (Calcein-AM), propidium iodide (PI) and2, 7-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China. Hoechst33342and1,1'-Dioctadecyl3,3,3',3'-Tetramethylindodicarbocyanine  Perchlorate  (DiD)  were  purchased  from  Beyotime  Biotechnology  Co.  Shanghai,  China. Hydroxyphenyl fluorescein (HPF) probe was purchased from Shanghai Maokang Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All reagents were analytical reagents and used without any further purification.

Synthesis of Fe3O4NPs: 110mL of ODE, 10mL of oleylamine and10mL of OA were added to a250mL three-neck flask and heated to100 ° C under Ar flow. Fe(CO)5 (1.2mL) was injected and the resulting solution heated to260 ° C under Ar flow, After30min, the solution was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. Then the Fe3O4NPs were washed with cyclohexane and ethylacetate twice and modified with PEG-VE.

Synthesis of PtFe  NRs: PtFe  NRs  were  prepared  by  following  a  reported  process. [45] Typically,  200mg  of  Pt(acac)2,  10mL  of oleylamine and10mL of ODE were loaded to a250mL three-neck flask and heated to100 ° C under Ar flow. Thereafter, 0.15mL of Fe(CO)5was injected, and the resulting solution was heated to160 ° C under Ar flow. After30min, the solution cooled naturally to room temperature. Then, the as-prepared PtFe NRs were washed with cyclohexane and ethylacetate twice and dispersed in10mL of cyclohexane. Finally, PtFe NRs were modified by mixing with PEG-VE solution (30mg mL -1 ) followed by centrifugation and washing with deionized water twice.

Synthesis of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs: PtFe NRs were prepared by following a reported process. [1]  Typically, 200mg of Pt(acac)2, 10mL of oleylamine and10mL of ODE were loaded to a250mL three-neck flask and heated to100 ° C under Ar flow. Thereafter, 0.15mL of Fe(CO)5was injected, and the resulting solution was heated to160 ° C under Ar flow. After30min, the solution cooled naturally to room temperature. Then, 100mL of ODE and10mL of OA were added and heated to100 ° C under Ar flow. Fe(CO)5 (1.2mL) was then added and the resulting solution was heated to260 ° C under Ar flow. After30min, the solution cooled naturally to room temperature. Then, the as-prepared PtFe@Fe3O4NRs were washed with cyclohexane and ethylacetate twice and dispersed in10mL of cyclohexane and modified with PEG-VE.

Characterizations of photothermal property: The photothermal property of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs was evaluated under808nm laser irradiation. PtFe@Fe3O4NRs were dispersed in PBS to obtain PtFe@Fe3O4dispersion with different concentrations (0, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200 µ g mL -1 ). The PtFe@Fe3O4dispersion were exposed to808nm laser irradiation for10min in a glass vial (2mL), PT-3S thermos-detector was used to detect the temperature changes of the solutions. The PtFe@Fe3O4NRs dispersion with a concentration of100  µ g  mL -1were  used  for  further  photothermal  property  characterization.  Temperature  changes  ( △ T)  of  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs dispersion  (100  μg  mL -1 )  under808nm  laser  irradiation  for30min,  then  following  by  naturally  cooling.  Real-time  temperature measurement of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs solution (100 μg mL -1 , 1mL) was performed under808nm laser irradiation for four cycles. Each cycle consisted of5min irradiation followed by a5min cooling phase. Similarly, the photothermal property of PtFe NRs and Fe3O4NPs were also evaluated.

POD-like activity of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs: The POD-like activity was assessed by UV-vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2600) after10min's reaction. 2 μL of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs, PtFe NRs or Fe3O4NPs (PtFe@Fe3O4: 100 μg mL -1 , PtFe: 32 μg mL -1 , Fe3O4: 68 μg mL1 ) was added into500 μL of NaAc buffer (pH=4.5) involved2 μL of TMB (20mg mL -1 ) and2 μL of H2O2 (30%). The photo-enhanced POD-like activity was carried out by exposing to808nm laser irradiation for3min during the10min's reaction procedure.

The  irradiation  time-dependent  photo-enhanced  POD-like  activity  of  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  was  measured  by  Lambda650S  UV-vis spectrophotometer. 1 μL PtFe@Fe3O4NRs was added into100 μL TMB liquid substrate system. In the beginning of the reaction, the above mixture was exposed to808nm laser (1.0W cm -2 ) for different times (5, 10, 20, 30, 40and50s) (n=3).Then the absorbance value at652nm was monitored during the5min's reaction procedure.

The steady-state kinetic assays were performed at0.1mL NaAc reaction buffer solution with10 μL PtFe@Fe3O4NRs solution (0.1mg mL -1 ), H2O2and TMB. The kinetic assays of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs with H2O2as the substrate were performed by adding10 μL TMB (20mg mL -1in DMSO) and different amounts of H2O2 (final working concentrations: 0.6, 0.3, 0.15, 0.075, 0.0375, 0.01875and0M). The kinetic assays of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs with TMB as the substrate were performed by adding6 μL H2O2 (30%) and different amounts of TMB (final working concentrations: 832.2, 665.76, 499.32, 332.88, 166.44, 83.22, 41.61and0 μM). The absorbance of all reactions were monitored at different reaction times, and the Michaelis-Menten constant was calculated according to the Michaelis-Menten saturation curve by GraphPad Prism7.0 (GraphPad Software).

CAT-like activity of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs: Oxygen electrode on Multi-Parameter Analyzer (JPSJ-606L, Leici China) was employed to detect the O2generation. 2mL nanoparticles solution (PtFe@Fe3O4: 100 μg mL -1 , PtFe: 32 μg mL -1 , Fe3O4: 68 μg mL -1 ) was added into10mL NaAc buffer containing200 μL30% H2O2. The generation of O2was measured at different time points. The photo-enhanced CAT-like activity of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs was carried out by adding808nm laser irradiation (1.0W cm -2 ) throughout the measurement process.

·OH and O2 · -  detection by electron spin resonance spectroscopy: 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) spin-trapping adduct was used to detect ·OH and O2 · -  generation. Specifically, 2 μL of H2O2, 10 μL of DMPO, 5 μL of NaAc buffer and2 μL of PtFe@Fe3O4


# SUPPORTING INFORMATION


NRs with a concentration of1mg mL -1were added into31 μL of DMSO solution for O2 · -  detection. 2 μL of H2O2, 10 μL of DMPO and2 μL of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs were added into36 μL of NaAc buffer for ·OH detection. All the samples were reacted for3min. For the NIR groups, 808nm laser irradiation (1.0W cm -2 , 3min) was employed.

Detection of H2O2consumption: Ammonium molybdate can form a yellow complex with H2O2, thus the residual H2O2in solution can be detected. 400 μL of ammonium molybdate (100mM) was incubated with300 μL of H2O2 (25mM) and50 μL of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs (100 μg mL -1 ) for5min reaction. NIR laser irradiation was employed for NIR group (3min) during the5min's reaction.

Cell culture: SW1990cells were cultured in complete Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM) containing10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1% penicillin, and2mM L-glutamine. The cell lines were incubated at constant temperature of37 ° C and5% CO2in the incubator, subcultured by adding1mL0.25% trypsin and complete DMEM.

In vitro cytotoxicity assays of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs: SW1990cells suspension with a concentration of10 4cells per well were seeded in96-well plates and incubated for24h, then medium was abandoned and different concentration of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs (0, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200 μg mL -1 ) dispersed in DMEM were added into96-well plates with SW1990cells, and incubated for another24h, then the MTT assay was used to test the cytotoxicity. After24h incubation with PtFe@Fe3O4NRs, SW1990cells were exposed to808nm laser (1.0W cm -2 , 3min per well) and the MTT assay was used to detect the photo-induced cytotoxicity.

In  vitro cytotoxicity  assays  for  SW1990cells  under  hypoxic  and  normoxic  conditions: SW1990cells  suspension  with  a concentration of10 4cells per well were seeded in96-well plates and incubated for24h, then medium was abandoned and different concentration of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs (0, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200 μg mL -1 ) dispersed in DMEM were added into96-well plates with SW1990cells, and incubated for another6h. Then, the plates were placed in a transparent box with air for normoxic condition and N2for hypoxic condition for2h, followed by808nm laser irradiation (1.0W cm -2 , 3min per well). Then the MTT assay was used to test the cytotoxicity.

Co-staining of SW1990cells with Calcein-AM and PI: The detection of living and dead cells was carried out by CLSM (Leica SP8, Germany). SW1990cells were seeded in CLSM-exclusive culture disk (35mm) and incubated for24h. PtFe@Fe3O4NRs with a concentration of100 μg mL -1dispersed in DMEM was incubated with cells for another24h, then washed with PBS twice before adding Calcein-AM and PI into wells. After incubation with Calcein-AM and PI for30min, cells were washed with PBS twice. PtFe@Fe3O4NIR group was exposed to808nm laser irradiation (808nm, 1.0W cm -2 ) for3min. The green and red fluorescence were observed by CLSM.

The generation of ROS in SW1990cells: The ROS generation in SW1990cells was observed on CLSM. SW1990cells were seeded in CLSM-exclusive culture disk (35mm) and incubated for24h. PtFe@Fe3O4NRs (100 μg mL -1 ) dispersed in DMEM was added into SW1990cells and incubated for another24h. Then the cells were washed with PBS twice, and DCFH-DA was added and incubated with cells for30min. Cells were then washed with PBS twice. PtFe@Fe3O4-NIR group was exposed to808nm laser irradiation (808nm, 1.0W cm -2 ) for3min. Finally, the ROS level was determined by detecting the fluorescence value of2,7-dichloro-fluorescein (DCF) which was obtained by the oxidation of DCFH. Then DCF fluorescence signal was captured by CLSM.

The specific detection of ·OH in SW1990cells: SW1990cells were seeded in CLSM-exclusive culture disk (35mm) and incubated for24h. PtFe@Fe3O4NRs (100 μg mL -1 ) dispersed in DMEM was added into SW1990cells and incubated for another12h. Then the cells were washed with PBS twice, and HPF probe was added and incubated for60min. Cells were then washed with PBS twice. PtFe@Fe3O4-NIR group was exposed to808nm laser irradiation (808nm, 1.0W cm -2 ) for3min. Finally, the ·OH level was captured by CLSM.

Flow cytometry analysis: SW1990cells were seeded in12-well plates with a concentration of8× 10 4and incubated for24h. After removing the DMEM, PtFe@Fe3O4NRs (100 μg mL -1 ) was added and incubated for another24h. Then the cells were washed with PBS twice, and incubated with DCFH-DA for30min. For NIR groups, cells were exposed to808nm laser irradiation for3min (1.0W cm -2 ) and incubated for another30min. Finally, above cells were collected and washed with PBS for further analysing by flow cytometry (MoFlo XDP, Beckman, CA, USA).

Co-staining of cell membrane and nucleus: SW1990cells were seeded in CLSM-exclusive culture disk (35mm) and incubated for24h. PtFe@Fe3O4NRs with a concentration of100 μg mL -1dispersed in DMEM was incubated with cells for another24h, then washed with PBS twice. PtFe@Fe3O4-NIR group was exposed to808nm laser irradiation (808nm, 1.0W cm -2 ) for3min. Then replaced the PBS with4% formaldehyde and incubated for20min, washed with PBS twice. 500 μL DiD (2 μM) was added and incubated with cells for20min washed with PBS twice then Hoechst33342was used to dye the nucleus for30min. finaly, the cells were washed with PBS twice and observed by CLSM.

The in vitro hemolysis percentages of PtFe@Fe3O4nanozyme: mice blood was first centrifugated for5min (3000rpm), then washed with PBS for3times. The collected RBCs were diluted50-fold. 0.5mL of RBC were mixed with0.5mL PtFe@Fe3O4NRs and incubated for2hours at37 ° C, then centrifugated for10min (10000rpm). PBS and D.I. water were used as the negative control and positive control,  respectively.  The  absorption  values  were  determined  at570nm.  The  percentage  of  hemolysis  was  calculated  following: hemolysis %=(sample absorbance-negative control absorbance)/(positive control absorbance-negative control absorbance)×100.

In vivo PA / CT imaging: SW1990tumor-bearing BALB/c nude mice were injected with PtFe@Fe3O4NRs (20mg kg -1 ) dispersed in PBS solution via tail vein and followed by PA imaging (MSOT inVision128, iThera medical, Germany) at different time point. For in vivo CT imaging, the images were obtained before and after intravenous injection of PtFe@Fe3O4NRs. All animal experimentation protocols were  approved  by  the  Animal  Ethics  Committee  of  the  Research  Center  for  Eco-Environmental  Sciences,  Chinese  Academy  of Sciences.

In vivo antitumor therapy: SW1990tumor-bearing BALB/c nude mice (8weeks) were randomly divided into4groups (8mice for each group):  PBS,  PBS-NIR,  PtFe@Fe3O4,  PtFe@Fe3O4-NIR.  For  PtFe@Fe3O4and  PtFe@Fe3O4-NIR  groups,  PtFe@Fe3O4NRs  with dosage of20mg kg -1were injected into SW1990tumor-bearing BALB/c nude mice via tail vein.
